Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation


Fitting a Poisson regression model via maximum likelihood to estimate the effect of Blueprinty’s software on patent awards.



David Nierman


May 18, 2026






No matching items

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Blueprinty is a small software company that develops tools specifically designed to help firms prepare and submit patent applications to the US Patent Office. Their marketing team wants to make the case that engineering firms using Blueprinty’s software have a higher rate of patent approval than those that do not.
Ideally, evaluating this claim would require panel data tracking each firm’s patent success rate both before and after adopting the software — a clean before-and-after comparison that would isolate the software’s effect. No such longitudinal data exists. Instead, Blueprinty has assembled a cross-sectional dataset of 1,500 mature (non-startup) engineering firms, capturing each firm’s number of patents awarded over the last five years, its regional location, its age since incorporation, and whether or not it is a Blueprinty customer.
The core difficulty is that Blueprinty’s customers are not randomly assigned. Firms that choose to adopt the software may differ systematically from those that do not — perhaps they are older, more established, or concentrated in regions with historically higher patent activity. Any raw difference in patent counts between customers and non-customers could therefore reflect those underlying firm characteristics rather than a genuine effect of the software. Untangling the software’s contribution from these pre-existing differences is the central analytical challenge of this study.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Blueprinty is a small software company that develops tools specifically designed to help firms prepare and submit patent applications to the US Patent Office. Their marketing team wants to make the case that engineering firms using Blueprinty’s software have a higher rate of patent approval than those that do not.
Ideally, evaluating this claim would require panel data tracking each firm’s patent success rate both before and after adopting the software — a clean before-and-after comparison that would isolate the software’s effect. No such longitudinal data exists. Instead, Blueprinty has assembled a cross-sectional dataset of 1,500 mature (non-startup) engineering firms, capturing each firm’s number of patents awarded over the last five years, its regional location, its age since incorporation, and whether or not it is a Blueprinty customer.
The core difficulty is that Blueprinty’s customers are not randomly assigned. Firms that choose to adopt the software may differ systematically from those that do not — perhaps they are older, more established, or concentrated in regions with historically higher patent activity. Any raw difference in patent counts between customers and non-customers could therefore reflect those underlying firm characteristics rather than a genuine effect of the software. Untangling the software’s contribution from these pre-existing differences is the central analytical challenge of this study.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Exploring the Data

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("blueprinty.csv")

customers = df[df["iscustomer"] == 1]["patents"]
non_customers = df[df["iscustomer"] == 0]["patents"]

mean_cust = customers.mean()
mean_non = non_customers.mean()

x_max = df["patents"].max() + 1
bins = range(0, x_max + 1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4), sharey=True)

axes[0].hist(non_customers, bins=bins, color="steelblue", edgecolor="white")
axes[0].axvline(mean_non, color="red", linestyle="--", label=f"Mean: {mean_non:.2f}")
axes[0].set_title("Non-Customers")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Patents Awarded (Last 5 Years)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Number of Firms")
axes[0].set_xlim(0, x_max)
axes[0].legend()

axes[1].hist(customers, bins=bins, color="darkorange", edgecolor="white")
axes[1].axvline(mean_cust, color="red", linestyle="--", label=f"Mean: {mean_cust:.2f}")
axes[1].set_title("Blueprinty Customers")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Patents Awarded (Last 5 Years)")
axes[1].set_xlim(0, x_max)
axes[1].legend()

fig.suptitle("Distribution of Patents by Customer Status", fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Blueprinty customers average 4.13 patents over the last five years, compared to 3.47 for non-customers — a meaningful gap on its face. Both distributions are right-skewed, with most firms clustered at low patent counts and a long tail of high-output firms. The customer distribution appears shifted rightward, suggesting customers tend to receive more patents. However, because customers are not randomly assigned, this difference may reflect underlying firm characteristics — such as age or region — rather than any effect of the software itself.

region_counts = df.groupby(["region", "iscustomer"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
region_counts.columns = ["Non-Customer", "Customer"]
region_pct = region_counts.div(region_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
region_pct.plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, color=["steelblue", "darkorange"], edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("Region Distribution by Customer Status (Share within Region)")
ax.set_xlabel("Region")
ax.set_ylabel("Proportion")
ax.set_xticklabels(region_pct.index, rotation=30, ha="right")
ax.legend(title="Status")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The Northeast stands out sharply: customers make up a majority of firms in that region, while all other regions are dominated by non-customers. This regional concentration matters because if Northeast firms also tend to win more patents for reasons unrelated to Blueprinty — stronger engineering ecosystems, proximity to patent attorneys, etc. — then the raw customer advantage in patent counts could be partly a Northeast effect rather than a software effect.

cust_age = df[df["iscustomer"] == 1]["age"]
non_cust_age = df[df["iscustomer"] == 0]["age"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4), sharey=True)

axes[0].hist(non_cust_age, bins=20, color="steelblue", edgecolor="white")
axes[0].axvline(non_cust_age.mean(), color="red", linestyle="--",
                label=f"Mean: {non_cust_age.mean():.1f}")
axes[0].set_title("Non-Customers")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Firm Age (Years)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Number of Firms")
axes[0].legend()

axes[1].hist(cust_age, bins=20, color="darkorange", edgecolor="white")
axes[1].axvline(cust_age.mean(), color="red", linestyle="--",
                label=f"Mean: {cust_age.mean():.1f}")
axes[1].set_title("Blueprinty Customers")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Firm Age (Years)")
axes[1].legend()

fig.suptitle("Distribution of Firm Age by Customer Status", fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The age distributions of customers and non-customers are broadly similar — both centered in the mid-twenties — but customers skew slightly older on average (26.9 years vs. 26.1 years for non-customers). Older firms have had more time to develop patent portfolios and internal processes for filing applications, so even a modest age difference could inflate the apparent patent advantage among customers. Taken together, the regional and age imbalances underscore why a simple comparison of means is insufficient: any rigorous assessment must control for these confounders.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
The number of patents awarded to each firm is a count — a non-negative integer. This rules out the Normal distribution as a natural generative model: a Normal can assign positive probability to negative or fractional values, neither of which makes sense for patent counts. The Poisson distribution is the standard starting point for non-negative integer outcomes. It is parameterized by a single rate \(\lambda > 0\), which represents the expected number of events, and it assigns probability only to the non-negative integers \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\). We therefore model each firm’s patent count \(Y_i\) as an independent draw from a Poisson distribution with rate \(\lambda\).
Given \(n\) independent observations \(Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n\) each drawn from a Poisson distribution with rate \(\lambda\), the probability mass function for a single observation is:
\[
f(Y_i \mid \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
Because the observations are independent, the joint likelihood is the product of the individual probabilities:
\[
L(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
Taking the natural log converts the product into a sum, which is much easier to work with analytically:
\[
\ell(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \log L(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ -\lambda + Y_i \log \lambda - \log(Y_i!) \right]
\]
Pulling out the terms that do not depend on the index \(i\):
\[
\ell(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = -n\lambda + \log(\lambda)\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log(Y_i!)
\]
The last term is a constant with respect to \(\lambda\) and plays no role in maximization. The MLE is found by differentiating and setting the derivative to zero, which yields \(\hat{\lambda} = \bar{Y}\) — the sample mean.

import numpy as np
from scipy.special import gammaln

def poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y):
    return np.sum(-lam + Y * np.log(lam) - gammaln(Y + 1))

Y = df["patents"].values
lambdas = np.linspace(0.1, 10, 500)
log_liks = [poisson_loglikelihood(l, Y) for l in lambdas]

mle_lambda = lambdas[np.argmax(log_liks)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(lambdas, log_liks, color="steelblue")
ax.axvline(mle_lambda, color="red", linestyle="--", label=f"MLE ≈ {mle_lambda:.2f}")
ax.set_xlabel("λ")
ax.set_ylabel("Log-Likelihood")
ax.set_title("Poisson Log-Likelihood over λ")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The curve rises steeply from low values of \(\lambda\), reaches a single peak, then falls off gradually to the right. The peak — marked by the red dashed line at \(\hat{\lambda} \approx\) 3.69 — is the maximum likelihood estimate: the value of \(\lambda\) that makes the observed patent counts most probable under the Poisson model. Reading the MLE off the plot is straightforward: it is simply the \(\lambda\) value directly below the highest point on the curve.

Analytical MLE
To find the MLE analytically, we differentiate the log-likelihood with respect to \(\lambda\) and set the result equal to zero. Starting from:
\[
\ell(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = -n\lambda + \log(\lambda)\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log(Y_i!)
\]
Taking the derivative:
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
\]
Setting this equal to zero and solving:
\[
\hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = \bar{Y}
\]
The MLE is simply the sample mean — a reassuring result, since \(\lambda\) is the mean of the Poisson distribution by definition.


Numerical MLE

from scipy.optimize import minimize_scalar

neg_loglik = lambda lam: -poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y)
result = minimize_scalar(neg_loglik, bounds=(0.01, 20), method="bounded")

numerical_mle = result.x
sample_mean = Y.mean()

print(f"Numerical MLE:  {numerical_mle:.4f}")
print(f"Sample mean:    {sample_mean:.4f}")

Numerical MLE:  3.6847
Sample mean:    3.6847


The numerical optimizer arrives at essentially the same answer as the sample mean (3.6847 vs. 3.6847), confirming the analytical result. This agreement is expected: both approaches are solving the same problem — one algebraically, one computationally — so they must converge to the same value.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Poisson Regression
The simple Poisson model assumed every firm shares the same rate \(\lambda\). That is too restrictive: older firms, Northeast firms, and Blueprinty customers all plausibly have different expected patent counts. Poisson regression relaxes this by letting each firm have its own rate \(\lambda_i\) that depends on its observed characteristics:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i) \qquad \text{where} \qquad \lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)
\]
Here \(X_i\) is a vector of covariates for firm \(i\) (age, region indicators, customer status) and \(\beta\) is a vector of coefficients to be estimated. The exponential function serves as the link between the linear predictor \(X_i'\beta\) — which can take any real value — and the rate parameter \(\lambda_i\), which must be strictly positive. By exponentiating, we guarantee \(\lambda_i > 0\) for any value of \(\beta\), making the model well-defined for count outcomes.
The log-likelihood function is updated to accept a covariate matrix \(X\) and coefficient vector \(\beta\), computing firm-specific rates via \(\lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)\):

def poisson_reg_loglik(beta, Y, X):
    eta = np.clip(X @ beta, -30, 30)
    return np.sum(-np.exp(eta) + Y * eta - gammaln(Y + 1))

The design matrix includes an intercept, firm age, age squared, region dummies (Midwest dropped as the reference category), and the customer indicator. Age squared is included because the relationship between firm age and patenting is unlikely to be linear — very young and very old firms may patent at different rates than a straight line would predict. One region must be omitted to avoid perfect collinearity: if we included all five region dummies, their sum would equal the intercept column, making \(X\) rank-deficient and the coefficients non-identifiable.

from scipy.optimize import minimize
from scipy.optimize._numdiff import approx_derivative
from scipy.linalg import inv

df['age2'] = df['age'] ** 2
regions = pd.get_dummies(df['region'], drop_first=True)

X = pd.concat([
    pd.Series(1, index=df.index, name='intercept'),
    df['age'],
    df['age2'],
    regions,
    df['iscustomer']
], axis=1).astype(float)

Y = df['patents'].values
X_mat = X.values

result = minimize(
    lambda b: -poisson_reg_loglik(b, Y, X_mat),
    x0=np.zeros(X_mat.shape[1]),
    method='BFGS'
)

beta_hat = result.x
H = approx_derivative(
    lambda b: approx_derivative(lambda b2: -poisson_reg_loglik(b2, Y, X_mat), b),
    beta_hat
)
se = np.sqrt(np.diag(inv(H)))

results_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Coefficient': beta_hat,
    'Std. Error': se
}, index=X.columns).round(5)

results_df

GLM Check

import statsmodels.api as sm

glm_fit = sm.GLM(Y, X_mat, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit()

glm_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'MLE Coef': beta_hat.round(5),
    'MLE SE': se.round(5),
    'GLM Coef': glm_fit.params.round(5),
    'GLM SE': glm_fit.bse.round(5)
}, index=X.columns)

glm_df

The coefficient estimates from both approaches are identical. The standard errors also match closely here because the numerical Hessian is computed via a fine-grained finite-difference approximation; in general, slight discrepancies can arise because sm.GLM uses the analytic (expected) information matrix while numerical optimizers approximate it, but with well-behaved data and a tight grid the two converge.


Interpretation
The age coefficient is positive (≈ 0.149) and the age-squared coefficient is negative (≈ −0.003), together describing a concave inverted-U relationship: patent output rises with firm maturity but at a decreasing rate and eventually plateaus. The region dummies are all small and statistically modest relative to their standard errors, suggesting that after controlling for age and customer status, regional differences in patent rates are limited.
The customer coefficient is 0.208 (SE ≈ 0.031), which is highly significant. Exponentiating gives \(e^{0.208} \approx 1.23\), meaning Blueprinty customers are estimated to receive about 23% more patents than otherwise identical non-customer firms. The sign is in the direction the marketing team hoped for, and the magnitude is economically meaningful. That said, this is still an observational estimate — unobserved firm-level differences not captured by age and region could still confound the result.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Effect of Blueprinty’s Software
Because Poisson regression is multiplicative in \(\lambda\), the customer coefficient (≈ 0.208) does not translate directly into “additional patents.” To produce a more interpretable estimate we use the counterfactual prediction approach: take the observed covariate matrix and flip every firm’s customer status to 0, then to 1, and compare predicted patent counts.

X0 = X_mat.copy()
X1 = X_mat.copy()
X0[:, -1] = 0  # set all firms to non-customer
X1[:, -1] = 1  # set all firms to customer

y0_hat = np.exp(X0 @ beta_hat)
y1_hat = np.exp(X1 @ beta_hat)

ate = (y1_hat - y0_hat).mean()
print(f"Average predicted patents (non-customer): {y0_hat.mean():.3f}")
print(f"Average predicted patents (customer):     {y1_hat.mean():.3f}")
print(f"Average treatment effect:                 {ate:.3f}")

Average predicted patents (non-customer): 3.436
Average predicted patents (customer):     4.229
Average treatment effect:                 0.793


Holding each firm’s age and region fixed at its observed values, switching customer status from 0 to 1 increases predicted patents by an average of 0.79 over five years — roughly 0.79 additional patents per firm. Put differently, the model estimates that Blueprinty’s software is associated with about one extra patent every six years for a typical firm in this sample.
This finding is consistent with Blueprinty’s marketing claim, but it does not establish causality. The analysis controls for age and region, yet several potentially important confounders remain unaddressed:

Internal R&D investment — firms that spend heavily on research are both more likely to patent and more likely to invest in specialized tools like Blueprinty’s software. Higher patent output may reflect R&D intensity rather than the software itself.
Patent attorney quality — firms with access to experienced IP counsel tend to file more successful applications. Such firms may also be more likely to adopt purpose-built patent software.
Firm size and revenue — larger firms have more resources to buy software subscriptions and more engineers generating patentable ideas. Size is not in the dataset, so its effect is absorbed into the error term.
Prior patent experience — firms already skilled at navigating the patent process may adopt Blueprinty to manage growing workloads, rather than using it to improve their odds. Selection based on prior success would inflate the apparent customer effect.

Without a randomized assignment of software access — or a credible natural experiment — we cannot rule out that these unobserved differences, rather than the software, explain the gap in patent counts.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
This is a walkthrough on leveraging a randomized controlled trial to apply classical frequentist statistics.
For this walkthrough, we will use a website’s newsletter as an example. The landing page includes a section where visitors can sign up to receive the newsletter. The goal is to increase the number of sign-ups by testing two different calls to action (CTAs).

CTA A: “Sign up for our FREE newsletter here!”


CTA B: “If you find this interesting, sign up for our newsletter here!”

To evaluate which CTA performs better, we will run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. We then compare the sign-up rates between the two groups to determine which message is more effective at driving conversions.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
This is a walkthrough on leveraging a randomized controlled trial to apply classical frequentist statistics.
For this walkthrough, we will use a website’s newsletter as an example. The landing page includes a section where visitors can sign up to receive the newsletter. The goal is to increase the number of sign-ups by testing two different calls to action (CTAs).

CTA A: “Sign up for our FREE newsletter here!”


CTA B: “If you find this interesting, sign up for our newsletter here!”

To evaluate which CTA performs better, we will run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. We then compare the sign-up rates between the two groups to determine which message is more effective at driving conversions.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
In this section, we will connect the A/B test to a more formal statistical framework.
Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we will model each outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter:

\(\boldsymbol{\pi}\) - probability of signing up.

Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their decision, we allow this probability to differ between groups:

\(\boldsymbol{\pi_A}\) Visitors who see CTA A sign-up probability.


\(\boldsymbol{\pi_B}\) Visitors who see CTA B sign-up probability. In either case, each outcome is binary: sign up (1) or not (0).

The main quantity we care about is the difference between the two sign-up probabilities:

\(\boldsymbol{\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B}\)

This captures how much better one CTA performs relative to the other.
To estimate this, we use the difference in sample means:

\(\boldsymbol{\hat{\theta}} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\)

Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is the proportion of visitors who signed up.

\(\boldsymbol{\bar{X}_A} = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\boldsymbol{\bar{X}_B} = \hat{\pi}_B\)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In the real world, we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) ahead of time; we would only estimate them after running an experiment. For this exercise, we will set these values ourselves so we can study how our estimator behaves when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).

import numpy as np
import polars as pl


# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18

# Number of samples per group
n = 1000

# Simulate Bernoulli draws
A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

# Create Polars DataFrame
data = pl.DataFrame({
    "group": ["A"] * n + ["B"] * n,
    "outcome": np.concatenate([A, B])
})

data.group_by("group").agg(
    pl.col("outcome").mean().alias("pi_hat"),
    pl.col("outcome").sum().alias("sign_ups"),
    pl.len().alias("n")
)


shape: (2, 4)



group
pi_hat

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
Samples are a strong way to represent the population.






Important



The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) tells us that as the sample size increases, the sample mean gets closer to the true population mean.


Because of LLN, we can be confident that our estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) as long as our sample is large enough.

# Set seed
np.random.seed(42) # 42 - also the answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta = pi_A - pi_B

# Different sample sizes
sample_sizes = (
    list(range(1, 30, 2)) +
    list(range(30, 200, 10)) +
    [500, 1000, 2000, 3000]
)

results = []

for n in sample_sizes:
    # Simulate draws
    A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

    # Element-wise differences
    diff = A - B

    # Estimate (mean of differences)
    theta_hat = diff.mean()

    results.append({
        "n": n,
        "theta_hat": theta_hat
    })



Show plotting code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Convert to Polars DataFrame
df = pl.DataFrame(results)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(df["n"], df["theta_hat"], marker="o")
plt.axhline(theta, linestyle="--", label="True difference (θ = 0.04)")

plt.xlabel("Sample size (n)")
plt.ylabel("Estimated difference (theta_hat)")
plt.title("Law of Large Numbers: Estimates Improve with Sample Size")
plt.xscale("log")
plt.legend()

plt.show()















Note



This plot shows that when the sample size is small, the estimated difference is pretty noisy and can move around a lot just due to random variation. As more observations are included, the estimate starts to stabilize and get closer to the true difference of 0.04 (since \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so \(0.22 - 0.18\)).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Now that we have some confidence in our estimator, the next question is how precise it actually is. Instead of only reporting a single estimate of \(\theta\), we also want to include a confidence interval to show the uncertainty around that estimate.
Bootstrapping is a way to understand how much our estimate might vary without needing a complicated formula. Instead of relying on theory, we use the data we already have and repeatedly resample from it (with replacement) to create many new “fake” datasets. For each of these, we calculate the statistic we care about. By looking at how those values change across resamples, we can get a sense of how much variability there is and use that to estimate the standard error.






Tip



Fun fact: The bootstrap method was introduced by statistician Bradley Efron in 1979 at Stanford University. The name comes from the phrase “pulling yourself up by your bootstraps,” since the method uses the data itself to learn about its own variability—no external assumptions needed.


First, we take one bootstrap resample from each group. This means we sample 1,000 observations with replacement from the CTA A group and 1,000 observations with replacement from the CTA B group. Using those resampled datasets, we compute one bootstrapped estimate of the difference in sign-up rates, \(\hat{\theta}^*\).

def bootstrap_theta(data, n=1000):
    A_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].to_numpy()
    B_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].to_numpy()

    A_star = np.random.choice(A_obs, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B_obs, size=n, replace=True)

    return A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()



Example call
theta_hat_star = bootstrap_theta(data)
theta_hat_star


np.float64(0.032)


Next, we repeat the resampling process 1,000 times. Each time, we call our bootstrap function to generate a new estimate of \(\hat{\theta}^*\). This gives us a distribution of bootstrapped estimates.

n_boot = 1_000

boot_theta = np.array([bootstrap_theta(data) for _ in range(n_boot)])

boot_theta[:10] # preview of first 10

array([0.041, 0.058, 0.039, 0.043, 0.062, 0.038, 0.042, 0.039, 0.026,
       0.012])


Once we have the 1,000 bootstrapped estimates, we can measure how much they vary. The standard deviation of these values is the bootstrapped standard error, which tells us how much uncertainty there is in our estimator

boot_se = boot_theta.std(ddof=1)
boot_se

np.float64(0.01770777242354905)


Finally, we compare the bootstrapped standard error to the analytical standard error. The analytical version comes from the standard formula for the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples.
\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

A_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].to_numpy()
B_obs = data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].to_numpy()

n = len(A_obs)

pi_hat_A = A_obs.mean()
pi_hat_B = B_obs.mean()

analytic_se = np.sqrt(
    (pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A) / n) +
    (pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B) / n)
)

comparison = pl.DataFrame({
    "method": ["Bootstrap SE", "Analytical SE"],
    "value": [boot_se, analytic_se]
})

comparison


shape: (2, 2)



method
value


str
f64




"Bootstrap SE"
0.017708


"Analytical SE"
0.017767












Note



The bootstrapped standard error and analytical standard error are extremely close to each other, with only a very small difference between them. This suggests that the bootstrap method is doing a good job estimating the variability of our estimator, even without relying on the analytical formula.



Confidence Interval
To construct a 95% confidence interval, we use the fact that our estimator is approximately normally distributed (we will explain why this is a reasonable assumption using the Central Limit Theorem in the next section). The value 1.96 comes from the standard normal distribution and represents the cutoff needed to capture the middle 95% of possible values. Using this, we take our estimate of \(\theta\) and add and subtract \(1.96 \times\) the standard error to get a range of plausible values for the true difference.

theta_hat = (
    data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].mean()
    - data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].mean()
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

(ci_lower, ci_upper)

(np.float64(0.0032927660498438677), np.float64(0.07270723395015614))




Show plotting code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Plot bootstrap distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(boot_theta, bins=30)

# Add vertical lines
plt.axvline(theta_hat, linestyle="-", linewidth=2, color="black", label="Estimate (θ̂)")
plt.axvline(ci_lower, linestyle="--", linewidth=2, color="red", label="Lower 95% CI")
plt.axvline(ci_upper, linestyle=":", linewidth=2, color="blue", label="Upper 95% CI")

plt.xlabel("Bootstrapped estimates of theta")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.title("Bootstrap Distribution with 95% Confidence Interval")
plt.legend()

plt.show()

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Important



The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) tells us that as our sample size gets larger, the distribution of our estimator starts to look approximately normal, even if the original data is not normally distributed.


In other words, regardless of the shape of the underlying population, the sample mean (and therefore the difference in sample means) will tend to follow a normal distribution when we have enough observations.

Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).



Show histogram code
def simulate_theta(pi_A, pi_B, n):
    A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

    return A.mean() - B.mean()

# Sample sizes to compare
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]

# Number of repeated simulations
n_sim = 1000

# Store results
theta_results = {}

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_hats = np.array([
        simulate_theta(pi_A, pi_B, n) for _ in range(n_sim)
    ])

    theta_results[n] = theta_hats




Show plotting code
all_theta = np.concatenate(list(theta_results.values()))
x_min = all_theta.min()
x_max = all_theta.max()

# Create 2x2 grid of histograms
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, n in zip(axes, sample_sizes):
    ax.hist(theta_results[n], bins=30, edgecolor="black")
    ax.axvline(0.04, linestyle="--", color="red", linewidth=2, label="True difference = 0.04")
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n}")
    ax.set_xlabel("Estimated difference (theta_hat)")
    ax.set_ylabel("Frequency")
    ax.set_xlim(x_min, x_max)

# Add one legend for the full figure
handles, labels = axes[0].get_legend_handles_labels()
fig.legend(handles, labels, loc="upper right")

plt.suptitle("Sampling Distribution of theta_hat at Different Sample Sizes", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









When the sample size is small, the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) looks pretty rough and can be a bit uneven due to randomness. As we increase the sample size, the distribution starts to smooth out and becomes more centered around the true value of 0.04. Eventually, it begins to take on a clear bell-shaped form. This is the Central Limit Theorem in action—larger samples lead to a more normal-looking distribution.

Move the slider below to change the sample size and see how the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) evolves as we include more observations.



Show slider code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True probabilities
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
true_theta = pi_A - pi_B

# Sample sizes the reader can explore
sample_sizes = [1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000]

# Number of simulated estimates per sample size
n_sim = 1000

# Precompute sampling distributions
theta_results = {}

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_hats = []
    for _ in range(n_sim):
        A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
        B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)
        theta_hats.append(A.mean() - B.mean())
    theta_results[n] = np.array(theta_hats)

# Common x-range for consistency across frames
all_theta = np.concatenate(list(theta_results.values()))
x_min = all_theta.min()
x_max = all_theta.max()

# Add a little padding so endpoint values like +1 are not lost on the boundary
pad = 0.05
bin_start = x_min - pad
bin_end = x_max + pad
bin_size = (bin_end - bin_start) / 30

# Build frames
frames = []
for n in sample_sizes:
    hist = go.Histogram(
        x=theta_results[n],
        nbinsx=30,
        name=f"n = {n}",
        xbins=dict(start=bin_start, end=bin_end, size=bin_size)
    )

    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            data=[hist],
            name=str(n),
            layout=go.Layout(
                title_text=f"Sampling Distribution of theta_hat (n = {n})"
            )
        )
    )

# Initial histogram
initial_n = sample_sizes[0]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Histogram(
            x=theta_results[initial_n],
            nbinsx=30,
            xbins=dict(start=bin_start, end=bin_end, size=bin_size)
        )
    ],
    layout=go.Layout(
        title=f"Sampling Distribution of theta_hat (n = {initial_n})",
        xaxis=dict(
            title="Estimated difference (theta_hat)",
            range=[bin_start, bin_end]
        ),
        yaxis=dict(title="Frequency"),
        shapes=[
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=true_theta,
                x1=true_theta,
                y0=0,
                y1=1,
                yref="paper",
                line=dict(color="red", dash="dash", width=3)
            )
        ],
        sliders=[
            {
                "currentvalue": {"prefix": "Sample size: "},
                "pad": {"t": 40},
                "steps": [
                    {
                        "args": [
                            [str(n)],
                            {
                                "frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": True},
                                "mode": "immediate",
                                "transition": {"duration": 0}
                            }
                        ],
                        "label": str(n),
                        "method": "animate"
                    }
                    for n in sample_sizes
                ]
            }
        ]
    ),
    frames=frames
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            








Note



Each individual observation is still either 0 or 1 (sign up or not). However, once we take multiple observations, we are no longer working with just 0s and 1s—we are working with averages.
For example, when \(n = 1\), a single draw might look like:
A: [1] → 1 B: [0] → 0
So the difference is: \[
\hat{\theta} = 1 - 0 = 1
\] In this case, there are only a few possible values, which is why the distribution looks very discrete.
Now, when \(n = 5\), we might observe something like:
A: [1, 0, 1, 0, 0] → 2/5 = 0.4 B: [0, 0, 1, 0, 0] → 1/5 = 0.2
The difference is now: \[
\hat{\theta} = 0.4 - 0.2 = 0.2
\]
Because we are averaging more observations, the estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) can take on many more possible values. As \(n\) increases, these values become more closely spaced, which is why the distribution starts to look smoother and less discrete.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.






Important



A hypothesis test is a method for using sample data to evaluate whether a claim about a population is likely to be true.


In this case, we start with a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\), which means that the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate and there is no real difference between them. The alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\) represents the idea that there is a difference between the two CTAs. The goal of the test is to use our sample data to determine whether there is enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that a meaningful difference exists.
We standardize \(\hat{\theta}\) to understand how unusual our estimate is under the null hypothesis. Since \(H_0\) assumes the true difference is 0, we subtract 0 and divide by the standard error to measure how many standard deviations our estimate is from 0: \[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]
By the Central Limit Theorem, \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately normally distributed for large samples, and under \(H_0\) it is centered at 0. Dividing by the standard error puts it on a standardized scale, so under the null we have \(z \dot\sim N(0,1)\).
Once we know this distribution, we can compute a p-value, which tells us how likely it is to observe a result this extreme if the null hypothesis were true.


Show slider code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.stats import norm

# Grid for standard normal curve
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 1000)
y = norm.pdf(x)

# z-values the slider will move through
z_values = np.round(np.arange(0.0, 3.1, 0.1), 1)

frames = []

for z in z_values:
    right_mask = x >= z
    left_mask = x <= -z
    p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(z))

    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            name=str(z),
            data=[
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x, y=y,
                    mode="lines",
                    name="Standard Normal",
                    line=dict(color="black")
                ),
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x[right_mask], y=y[right_mask],
                    mode="lines",
                    fill="tozeroy",
                    name="Right tail",
                    line=dict(color="red")
                ),
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x[left_mask], y=y[left_mask],
                    mode="lines",
                    fill="tozeroy",
                    name="Left tail",
                    line=dict(color="red")
                )
            ],
            layout=go.Layout(
                title=f"Standard Normal Distribution with z = {z:.1f} and p-value = {p_value:.4f}",
                shapes=[
                    dict(
                        type="line",
                        x0=z, x1=z, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(z),
                        line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
                    ),
                    dict(
                        type="line",
                        x0=-z, x1=-z, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(-z),
                        line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
                    )
                ]
            )
        )
    )

# Initial value
z0 = z_values[0]
right_mask = x >= z0
left_mask = x <= -z0
p0 = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(z0))

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Scatter(
            x=x, y=y,
            mode="lines",
            name="Standard Normal",
            line=dict(color="black")
        ),
        go.Scatter(
            x=x[right_mask], y=y[right_mask],
            mode="lines",
            fill="tozeroy",
            name="Right tail",
            line=dict(color="red")
        ),
        go.Scatter(
            x=x[left_mask], y=y[left_mask],
            mode="lines",
            fill="tozeroy",
            name="Left tail",
            line=dict(color="red")
        )
    ],
    layout=go.Layout(
        title=f"Standard Normal Distribution with z = {z0:.1f} and p-value = {p0:.4f}",
        xaxis=dict(title="z", range=[-4, 4]),
        yaxis=dict(title="Density"),
        shapes=[
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=z0, x1=z0, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(z0),
                line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
            ),
            dict(
                type="line",
                x0=-z0, x1=-z0, y0=0, y1=norm.pdf(-z0),
                line=dict(color="blue", dash="dash", width=2)
            )
        ],
        sliders=[
            {
                "currentvalue": {"prefix": "z-value: "},
                "pad": {"t": 40},
                "steps": [
                    {
                        "args": [
                            [str(z)],
                            {"frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": True},
                             "mode": "immediate",
                             "transition": {"duration": 0}}
                        ],
                        "label": str(z),
                        "method": "animate"
                    }
                    for z in z_values
                ]
            }
        ]
    ),
    frames=frames
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            


The plot above shows the standard normal distribution under the null hypothesis. The dashed vertical lines represent the value of the test statistic \(z\) and its negative. The shaded areas in the tails represent the probability of observing a value at least this extreme if the null hypothesis were true. This shaded area is the p-value.
As the value of \(z\) moves farther away from 0, the shaded area gets smaller. This means the p-value decreases, which provides stronger evidence against the null hypothesis. In other words, large values of \(|z|\) correspond to results that would be unlikely to occur by chance if \(H_0\) were true.
In practice, we compare the p-value to a chosen significance level (often denoted \(\alpha\)) to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis. Common thresholds are:









Significance Level (\(\alpha\))
Confidence Level
Approx. Critical \(|z|\)
Interpretation




0.10
90%
1.64
Weak evidence against \(H_0\)


0.05
95%
1.96
Moderate evidence against \(H_0\)


0.01
99%
2.58
Strong evidence against \(H_0\)



If the p-value is less than \(\alpha\), we reject the null hypothesis. Otherwise, we do not reject it.
Another way to think about this is through confidence intervals. For example, a 95% confidence interval corresponds to a significance level of \(\alpha = 0.05\). If the interval does not include 0, that is equivalent to rejecting the null hypothesis at the 5% level.
This is sometimes referred to as a “t-test,” but that is not exactly what we are doing here. The classical t-test comes from a setting where the data are normally distributed and the variance is unknown, so we estimate it from the data. In that case, the resulting test statistic follows an exact t-distribution.
In our setting, however, the data are Bernoulli (0 or 1), not normally distributed, so there is no exact t-distribution result. Instead, we rely on the Central Limit Theorem, which tells us that our estimator is approximately normally distributed when the sample size is large. Because of this, our test statistic is better thought of as following an approximate standard normal distribution.
In practice, this distinction does not matter much for large samples, since the t-distribution and the standard normal distribution are very similar. However, it is still important to understand where each result comes from and why we are using this approximation. :::
Use the slider below to change the degrees of freedom and see how the t-distribution compares to the standard normal distribution.


Show slider code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.stats import norm, t

# x-grid
x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 1000)

# standard normal density
normal_y = norm.pdf(x)

# degrees of freedom to show
df_values = [1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100]

frames = []

for df in df_values:
    t_y = t.pdf(x, df)

    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            name=str(df),
            data=[
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x,
                    y=normal_y,
                    mode="lines",
                    name="Standard Normal",
                    line=dict(color="black", width=3)
                ),
                go.Scatter(
                    x=x,
                    y=t_y,
                    mode="lines",
                    name=f"t-distribution (df = {df})",
                    line=dict(color="purple", width=3, dash="dash")
                )
            ],
            layout=go.Layout(
                title=f"t-Distribution vs. Standard Normal (df = {df})"
            )
        )
    )

# initial df
df0 = df_values[0]
t0_y = t.pdf(x, df0)

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Scatter(
            x=x,
            y=normal_y,
            mode="lines",
            name="Standard Normal",
            line=dict(color="black", width=3)
        ),
        go.Scatter(
            x=x,
            y=t0_y,
            mode="lines",
            name=f"t-distribution (df = {df0})",
            line=dict(color="purple", width=3, dash="dash")
        )
    ],
    layout=go.Layout(
        title=f"t-Distribution vs. Standard Normal (df = {df0})",
        xaxis=dict(title="Value", range=[-5, 5]),
        yaxis=dict(title="Density"),
        sliders=[
            {
                "currentvalue": {"prefix": "Degrees of freedom: "},
                "pad": {"t": 40},
                "steps": [
                    {
                        "args": [
                            [str(df)],
                            {
                                "frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": True},
                                "mode": "immediate",
                                "transition": {"duration": 0}
                            }
                        ],
                        "label": str(df),
                        "method": "animate"
                    }
                    for df in df_values
                ]
            }
        ]
    ),
    frames=frames
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            








Note



When the degrees of freedom are small, the t-distribution has heavier tails, but as the degrees of freedom increase, it becomes almost identical to the normal distribution.


Using our simulated data, we compute the test statistic by standardizing our estimate of \(\hat{\theta}\) and then calculate the corresponding p-value using the standard normal distribution.

from scipy.stats import norm

# Estimate
theta_hat = (
    data.filter(pl.col("group") == "A")["outcome"].mean()
    - data.filter(pl.col("group") == "B")["outcome"].mean()
)

# Test statistic
z_stat = theta_hat / analytic_se

# Two-sided p-value
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

round(float(theta_hat), 3), round(float(z_stat), 3), round(float(p_value), 3)

(0.038, 2.139, 0.032)


The estimated difference in sign-up rates is 0.038, with a test statistic of 2.14 and a p-value of 0.032. This means that, assuming the null hypothesis is true, there is about 3.245% chance of observing a result this extreme purely due to random variation.
Since the p-value is below the 0.05 significance level, we reject the null hypothesis. This suggests that there is statistically significant evidence of a difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs, with CTA A performing better than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression.
Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.



It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran can also be written as a simple linear regression. This is useful because it shows that hypothesis testing and regression are closely connected. In practice, this means we can use the same regression tools and software to answer the same question, and that framework can easily be extended to more complicated settings later on.
To set this up, we combine the CTA A and CTA B observations into one dataset. For each visitor, we define an outcome variable \(Y_i\) that equals 1 if the visitor signed up and 0 otherwise. We also define an indicator variable \(D_i\) that equals 1 if the visitor saw CTA A and 0 if the visitor saw CTA B. We then fit the regression \[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\] which lets us estimate the difference in average sign-up rates between the two groups.
We can interpret the coefficients in this regression in a straightforward way. First, \(\beta_0\) represents the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\). Since \(D_i = 0\) corresponds to CTA B, this is just the average sign-up rate for group B, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
Next, when \(D_i = 1\) (CTA A), the expected value of \(Y_i\) becomes \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\). This is the average sign-up rate for group A, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Putting this together, \(\beta_1\) captures the difference between the two groups. Specifically, \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\). This means the estimated coefficient \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is exactly the same as the difference in sample means, \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
We can now fit this regression using our simulated data and compare the results to the two-sample test we ran earlier.

import statsmodels.api as sm

# Create indicator variable D (1 for A, 0 for B)
df_reg = data.with_columns(
    (pl.col("group") == "A").cast(int).alias("D")
)

# Convert to pandas for statsmodels
df_reg_pd = df_reg.to_pandas()

# Define variables
Y = df_reg_pd["outcome"]
X = sm.add_constant(df_reg_pd["D"])  # adds intercept

# Fit regression
model = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit()

model.summary()


OLS Regression Results

0.002

1998.

nonrobust











coef
std err
t
P>|t|
[0.025
0.975]


const
0.1780
0.013
14.161

Notes:[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.


From the regression results, the coefficient on \(D_i\) is 0.038. This means that showing CTA A increases the sign-up rate by about 3.8 percentage points compared to CTA B. This matches exactly with the difference in sample means we computed earlier.
The standard error for this estimate is approximately 0.018, which leads to a t-statistic of 2.14 and a p-value of 0.033. Since the p-value is below the 0.05 significance level, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is statistically significant evidence of a difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
These results are very consistent with the two-sample test we ran earlier. The estimated effect, test statistic, and p-value are nearly identical. Any small differences come from how the standard error is calculated: the regression uses a pooled variance, while the two-sample approach allows for separate variances. In this case, the difference is minimal.
Overall, both approaches lead to the same conclusion: CTA A performs better than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
This is problematic because the 5% false positive rate only applies to a single test. Each time we peek at the data, we are effectively running another test. This creates multiple opportunities to incorrectly reject the null hypothesis. As a result, the overall probability of making at least one false positive across all the peeks becomes much larger than 5%.
We can demonstrate this with a simulation.

import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm

np.random.seed(42)

# True probabilities under the null
pi_A = 0.20
pi_B = 0.20

n_total = 1000
peek_points = np.arange(100, 1001, 100)
n_sim = 10000

false_positive_count = 0

for _ in range(n_sim):
    A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n_total)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n_total)

    significant = False

    for n in peek_points:
        A_n = A[:n]
        B_n = B[:n]

        theta_hat = A_n.mean() - B_n.mean()

        # standard error (pooled approximation)
        se = np.sqrt(
            (A_n.mean() * (1 - A_n.mean()) / n) +
            (B_n.mean() * (1 - B_n.mean()) / n)
        )

        z = theta_hat / se if se > 0 else 0
        p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))

        if p_value < 0.05:
            significant = True
            break

    if significant:
        false_positive_count += 1

false_positive_rate = false_positive_count / n_sim
false_positive_rate

0.1864


The simulation shows that the empirical false positive rate under peeking is 0.1864, or about 18.6%. This is much higher than the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single hypothesis test.
This happens because each peek at the data creates another opportunity to reject the null hypothesis by chance alone. Even if there is truly no difference between the two CTAs, repeatedly testing the data makes it much more likely that at least one of those tests will produce a statistically significant result.






Warning



In practice, this means that peeking can lead us to declare a winner too early and make decisions based on noise rather than a real effect. For A/B testing, this is why it is important to either commit to a fixed sample size in advance or use methods that are specifically designed for sequential testing.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
In 1999, Joshua Angrist and Victor Lavy published a landmark study asking a deceptively simple question: does putting students in smaller classes actually help them learn? The challenge is that this is genuinely hard to answer with observational data. Schools cannot randomly assign students to class sizes, and the factors that determine class size — school wealth, district resources, demographics — are also correlated with student outcomes. A simple comparison of students in large versus small classes will confound the effect of class size with all of these background differences.
Angrist and Lavy’s breakthrough was recognizing that Israeli school policy created a natural experiment. Israeli public schools are required to follow Maimonides’ rule: no class may have more than 40 students. When a grade’s enrollment reaches 41, the school must split into two classes; at 81, three; at 121, four; and so on. This generates sharp, predictable discontinuities in class size at enrollment thresholds of 40, 80, 120, and 160. The key insight is that students just below and just above each threshold are otherwise similar — they attend the same school and face the same local conditions — but end up in very differently sized classes purely because of where enrollment happened to fall.
The predicted class size implied by Maimonides’ rule is:
\[\text{fsc} = \frac{\text{enrollment}}{\lfloor (\text{enrollment} - 1)/40 \rfloor + 1}\]
This nonlinear function of enrollment creates quasi-experimental variation in class size. By comparing outcomes near the thresholds — and by controlling for smooth enrollment effects — the authors can attribute differences in achievement specifically to the discontinuous shifts in class size, rather than to correlated socioeconomic factors.
This post replicates three core pieces of the paper using Israel’s 1991 fourth-grade data: (1) Figure 1 Panel B, showing how actual class sizes track Maimonides’ rule; (2) the naive OLS estimates from Table II, which serve as a confounded baseline; and (3) the reduced-form RDD estimates from Table III Panel A, which use the rule as an instrument.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
In 1999, Joshua Angrist and Victor Lavy published a landmark study asking a deceptively simple question: does putting students in smaller classes actually help them learn? The challenge is that this is genuinely hard to answer with observational data. Schools cannot randomly assign students to class sizes, and the factors that determine class size — school wealth, district resources, demographics — are also correlated with student outcomes. A simple comparison of students in large versus small classes will confound the effect of class size with all of these background differences.
Angrist and Lavy’s breakthrough was recognizing that Israeli school policy created a natural experiment. Israeli public schools are required to follow Maimonides’ rule: no class may have more than 40 students. When a grade’s enrollment reaches 41, the school must split into two classes; at 81, three; at 121, four; and so on. This generates sharp, predictable discontinuities in class size at enrollment thresholds of 40, 80, 120, and 160. The key insight is that students just below and just above each threshold are otherwise similar — they attend the same school and face the same local conditions — but end up in very differently sized classes purely because of where enrollment happened to fall.
The predicted class size implied by Maimonides’ rule is:
\[\text{fsc} = \frac{\text{enrollment}}{\lfloor (\text{enrollment} - 1)/40 \rfloor + 1}\]
This nonlinear function of enrollment creates quasi-experimental variation in class size. By comparing outcomes near the thresholds — and by controlling for smooth enrollment effects — the authors can attribute differences in achievement specifically to the discontinuous shifts in class size, rather than to correlated socioeconomic factors.
This post replicates three core pieces of the paper using Israel’s 1991 fourth-grade data: (1) Figure 1 Panel B, showing how actual class sizes track Maimonides’ rule; (2) the naive OLS estimates from Table II, which serve as a confounded baseline; and (3) the reduced-form RDD estimates from Table III Panel A, which use the rule as an instrument.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
Main Analysis

Figure 1 Panel B: Class Size Tracks Maimonides’ Rule
The first step is to verify that Maimonides’ rule actually shapes class sizes in practice. The figure below replicates Figure 1 Panel B from the paper. Each circle is the average actual class size for all schools with a given enrollment count; the red line is the size predicted by Maimonides’ rule.








Figure 1: Replication of Figure 1 Panel B (Angrist and Lavy 1999). Circles show average actual class size by initial enrollment count; the red step function shows the class size predicted by Maimonides’ rule. Dashed vertical lines mark discontinuity thresholds at enrollments of 40, 80, 120, and 160.





The figure shows the classic staircase pattern. Predicted class size drops sharply whenever enrollment crosses a multiple of 40, then climbs again as enrollment rises within the next segment. Actual class sizes track this pattern closely. The alignment between circles and the red line confirms that Maimonides’ rule is a strong predictor of actual class size — the foundation for using it as an instrument.


Naive OLS: Table II (Columns 7 and 10)
Before exploiting the rule, Angrist and Lavy document what a naive regression produces. These regressions simply regress average test scores on actual class size, with no controls and no attempt to address endogeneity. The results are shown below.


Table II (4th grade): Naive OLS — Actual Class Size on Test Scores




Col 7 — Reading
Col 10 — Math




Coefficient on class size
0.141***
0.221***


Std. error
(0.035)
(0.039)


Observations
2049
2049


R²
0.013
0.025



*** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1. Standard errors clustered by school. Published benchmarks: Col 7 = 0.141, Col 10 = 0.221.


Both coefficients are positive: a larger class is associated with higher test scores. This is the wrong sign if we believe class size harms learning, and it is exactly the confound the paper warns about. Larger schools in Israel tend to be urban schools in more affluent municipalities, which have higher baseline achievement for reasons entirely unrelated to class size. The naive regression mistakenly credits large classes for this advantage.


Reduced-Form RDD: Table III Panel A (Columns 7, 9, 11)
The paper’s preferred approach replaces actual class size with fsc — the class size predicted by Maimonides’ rule — as the explanatory variable. This is a reduced-form regression in the RDD framework: instead of using the endogenous actual class size, we use a function of enrollment that only shifts class sizes through the rule’s discontinuities. The main control is tipuach, the fraction of disadvantaged students, which absorbs socioeconomic differences across schools.


Table III Panel A (4th grade): Reduced-Form RDD — fsc on Class Size and Scores




Col 7 — Class Size
Col 9 — Reading
Col 11 — Math




Coefficient on fsc
0.772***
-0.085***
0.038


Std. error
(0.023)
(0.031)
(0.040)


Observations
2049
2049
2049


R²
0.561
0.311
0.203



*** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1. Standard errors clustered by school. Published benchmarks: Col 7 = 0.772, Col 9 = −0.085, Col 11 = 0.038.


Three results stand out:

First stage (Col 7): fsc strongly predicts actual class size. A one-unit increase in predicted class size raises actual class size by roughly 0.77. This confirms the instrument’s relevance — Maimonides’ rule genuinely shifts class sizes.
Reading (Col 9): The coefficient on fsc is negative. Schools pushed into larger classes by the rule score lower on reading. This is the expected sign for a harmful effect of class size.
Math (Col 11): The coefficient is small and near zero, suggesting weaker or noisier effects on math achievement for fourth graders.



Comparing Naive OLS vs. Reduced-Form RDD


Summary: Naive OLS vs. RDD-Based Estimates



Estimate type
Reading
Math




Naive OLS (coeff on actual class size)
0.141
0.221


Reduced-form fsc (coeff on Maimonides' rule)
-0.085
0.038


Informal IV = Reduced Form ÷ First Stage
-0.110
0.049





The pattern is stark. For reading, the naive OLS estimate is positive (around +0.14), while the informal IV estimate — the reduced-form coefficient divided by the first stage — is negative (roughly −0.11). For math, a large positive naive coefficient of about +0.22 shrinks to near zero once we use the instrumental variable strategy.
The reason is selection bias. Larger Israeli schools tend to serve wealthier urban communities with higher baseline achievement. A naive regression attributes this socioeconomic advantage to large class sizes, yielding a spurious positive relationship. Maimonides’ rule generates variation in class size that is driven purely by enrollment falling just above or below a threshold — a shift unrelated to school wealth. When we isolate this source of variation, the confound disappears and a negative (or much smaller) effect emerges.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
section: Something Additional: Visualizing the Upward Bias
text:
Something Additional: Visualizing the Upward Bias
The bar chart below makes the confounding concrete. For each subject, it shows the naive OLS estimate alongside the informal IV estimate derived from the RDD design.








Figure 2: Naive OLS vs. informal IV estimates of the effect of class size on test scores (4th grade, Israel 1991). The informal IV is computed as the reduced-form coefficient on fsc divided by the first-stage coefficient on fsc. The sign reversal for reading illustrates how upward bias from socioeconomic sorting distorts the naive estimate.





For reading, the naive estimate and the IV estimate are not just different in magnitude — they are opposite in sign. Naive OLS says larger classes are associated with higher scores (+0.14). The IV estimate says larger classes lower scores (about −0.11). This is a textbook example of omitted-variable bias: once you control for the endogenous sorting of students into schools, the naive positive estimate completely reverses.
For math, the story is similar in direction — the large positive naive coefficient (+0.22) shrinks dramatically — but the IV estimate stays mildly positive and small (+0.05), suggesting the effect of class size on math may be genuinely weaker or noisier for fourth graders.
A few caveats about this replication are worth noting. The original paper uses Moulton (1986) corrected standard errors, which account for within-school clustering in a specific way; this replication uses the standard Liang–Zeger cluster-robust sandwich estimator, so standard errors may differ slightly even when point estimates match. Additionally, the paper’s preferred estimates in Table IV use formal two-stage least squares with richer controls; the informal Wald estimates shown here are illustrative. Despite these caveats, the main message of the paper comes through clearly: naive comparisons of class sizes are misleading, and the discontinuities created by Maimonides’ rule provide a credible way to recover a causal estimate of class size on student achievement.

Data source: Angrist and Lavy (1999), “Using Maimonides’ Rule to Estimate the Effect of Class Size on Scholastic Achievement,” Quarterly Journal of Economics 114(2): 533–575. Data obtained from the authors’ replication files.
